retrieved_skills.skill_text: The street architecture shows houses with flat roofs and walled facades, common in Mexican cities.
The sidewalk and curb construction style, with its painted edge and sloped access points, is typical for small towns in southern Mexico.
Combining the tropical vegetation, suburban-rural setting, architecture, curb and sidewalk design, I estimate this image is in Mexico, specifically a small town near Mérida, Yucatán.
The street is paved with asphalt, and the absence of painted lane lines on a residential street fits many small towns in these regions.
The architecture features flat-roofed, single-story houses with pastel colors, common in rural Mexico and Central America.
The condition of the road and sidewalk as well as the electric pole style matches rural settings in Mexico, especially Yucatán, Campeche, or Tabasco.
The electrical wiring is above ground and strung across the street in a web formation, very typical of Mexico.
The street layout and the presence of a detached single-family home structure in a tidy residential neighborhood further point toward France.
The sky is clear and the lighting is very intense, supporting a location in central or northern Mexico.
Based on the landscape, infrastructure, and road markings, this scene is very likely in Argentina, specifically in the Córdoba region near the city of Córdoba.
Overall, the combination of landscape, climate, and road quality closely matches what is found in rural parts of the Caribbean, specifically in Cuba.
The red tile roofs and the style of the doors and windows fit with towns in Boyacá or Cundinamarca departments in Colombia.
